column4: Question
column6: 1 - Lagging
column7: 2 - Developing
column8: 3 - Proficient
column9: 4 - Exemplary

column4: How consistently does the Frontend Head deliver completed features and UI updates on the committed schedule?
column6: Releases frequently miss target dates; delivery predictability is unreliable.
column7: Most releases miss deadlines; sprint velocity is inconsistent.
column8: Releases typically meet committed dates with occasional minor delays.
column9: All features ship on schedule; sprint velocity is predictable and well-forecasted.

column4: How effectively does the team maintain high-quality user interfaces while optimizing Core Web Vitals (LCP, FID, CLS)?
column6: Core Web Vitals scores are poor (red in Lighthouse); UI quality issues block user experience.
column7: Core Web Vitals are inconsistent; some pages meet targets while others lag significantly.
column8: Most Core Web Vitals targets are met; UI quality issues are caught and resolved quickly.
column9: All pages maintain excellent Core Web Vitals scores and UI quality is consistently high.

column4: Is the 11-person team allocated effectively with reasonable workload distribution and minimal burnout?
column6: Team is severely overallocated; frequent context-switching; high burnout signals.
column7: Team allocation is uneven; some members are overloaded while others are underutilized.
column8: Workload is generally balanced across the team with occasional spikes handled well.
column9: Team workload is optimally distributed; members have room for growth and reduce burnout.

column4: How well does the team leverage Vite, CI/CD automation, and testing frameworks to improve development velocity?
column6: Build tooling is outdated; CI/CD is manual and slow; tests are minimal or ignored.
column7: Some build optimization efforts underway; CI/CD has gaps; test coverage is inconsistent.
column8: Build tooling is well-configured; CI/CD pipeline is reliable; automated tests provide confidence.
column9: Build tooling is optimized; CI/CD is fast and robust; automated testing enables rapid iteration.

column4: Does the Frontend Head maintain rigorous quality gates before production releases?
column6: Releases go out without proper testing or sign-off; quality is unpredictable.
column7: Release checklist exists but is inconsistently applied; occasional regressions in production.
column8: Release gates are enforced; most issues are caught before production.
column9: Release gates are rigorous; all releases are thoroughly validated before deployment.

column4: How clearly and proactively does the Frontend Head communicate team progress, blockers, and status to leadership?
column6: Status is unclear or absent; stakeholders are frequently surprised by delays or issues.
column7: Status updates are irregular; stakeholders sometimes lack clarity on progress.
column8: Weekly status updates are clear and highlight key blockers and progress.
column9: Stakeholders have clear visibility into progress, risks, and upcoming deliverables.

column4: How effectively does the team respond to production frontend incidents and prevent recurrence?
column6: Incidents cause extended outages; no post-mortems or preventative measures.
column7: Incidents are addressed eventually; RCA is inconsistent or superficial.
column8: Incidents are resolved quickly; clear RCA identifies root causes.
column9: Incidents are rare; when they occur, RCA is thorough and preventative fixes are implemented.

column4: How rigorously does the team follow specification requirements and maintain code review standards?
column6: PRs are merged without review; specs are ignored; code quality is poor.
column7: PRs sometimes lack review; spec alignment is spotty.
column8: PRs are consistently reviewed and specs are generally followed.
column9: All PRs meet review standards; specs drive implementation; design system adherence is high.

column4: How effectively does the Frontend Head identify and remove blockers to keep the team unblocked?
column6: Team is frequently blocked on design decisions, backend dependencies, or tooling issues.
column7: Blockers exist for days before resolution; team velocity suffers.
column8: Blockers are typically resolved within a day; team flow is good.
column9: Blockers are identified early and resolved quickly; team flow is seamless.

column4: How well are React components architected and documented for long-term maintainability?
column6: React patterns are inconsistent; no documentation; technical debt is high.
column7: React code has some patterns; documentation is minimal.
column8: React components follow consistent patterns; documentation covers most components.
column9: React architecture is clean and scalable; documentation is comprehensive and kept current.

column4: How effectively does the team conduct peer code reviews and mentor junior developers?
column6: Code reviews are rare or superficial; knowledge stays siloed.
column7: Code reviews happen but lack depth; mentorship is informal.
column8: Code reviews are constructive; junior developers receive regular guidance.
column9: Code reviews drive learning; mentorship is structured and intentional.

column4: How proactively does the team identify, prioritize, and address technical debt?
column6: Technical debt is untracked and growing; it impedes new feature development.
column7: Technical debt exists but is rarely addressed; it accumulates.
column8: Technical debt is tracked and addressed in regular sprints.
column9: Technical debt is actively managed; refactoring is built into sprint planning.

column4: How well does the team share knowledge and grow their technical capabilities?
column6: Knowledge is siloed; no training or learning opportunities; skills stagnate.
column7: Some knowledge sharing occurs; learning is ad-hoc.
column8: Regular knowledge sharing sessions; team members are upskilling.
column9: Structured learning program; team members mentor each other; skills are continuously evolving.

column4: Does the team feel psychologically safe to voice concerns, ask questions, and suggest improvements?
column6: Team is afraid to speak up; feedback is one-way; morale is low.
column7: Some team members hold back; feedback is inconsistent.
column8: Team feels mostly safe; feedback is two-way and constructive.
column9: Team has strong psychological safety; all members actively contribute ideas and feedback.

column4: Are team members engaged and staying with the organization?
column6: High turnover; disengagement is evident.
column7: Some turnover; engagement is mixed.
column8: Low turnover; team members are generally engaged.
column9: Team retention is high; engagement scores are strong; team members advocate for the role.

column4: How effectively is frontend knowledge captured and shared with other teams (Creative Design, Backend)?
column6: No cross-team knowledge sharing; frontend expertise is not leveraged.
column7: Occasional cross-team discussions; knowledge is not documented.
column8: Regular knowledge sharing with other teams; some documentation exists.
column9: Frontend team is a trusted knowledge hub; expertise is actively shared and documented.

column4: How well does the Frontend Head translate product vision into clear team goals and purpose?
column6: Team doesn't understand the vision or strategy.
column7: Vision is vague; team alignment is weak.
column8: Vision is clear; team understands the direction.
column9: Vision is compelling; team is aligned and motivated by the purpose.

column4: How effectively does the Frontend Head drive strategic frontend initiatives and measure their impact?
column6: No strategic initiatives; impact is unmeasured.
column7: Occasional initiatives launched; impact is unclear.
column8: Strategic initiatives are defined and their impact is measured.
column9: Major initiatives are delivered; clear metrics show business impact.

column4: Does the Frontend Head prioritize work based on identified risks to performance, accessibility, and user experience?
column6: Work is prioritized randomly; risks are not identified or mitigated.
column7: Some risk awareness; prioritization is sometimes reactive.
column8: Risks are identified and prioritized appropriately.
column9: Risks are systematically identified and prioritized; contingency plans are in place.

column4: How clearly does the Frontend Head articulate trade-offs between speed, quality, scope, and technical debt?
column6: Trade-offs are not discussed; decisions are unclear.
column7: Trade-offs are acknowledged but not always transparent.
column8: Trade-offs are clearly discussed before decisions.
column9: Trade-offs are explicitly framed and communicated to stakeholders.

column4: How quickly and thoroughly does the team incorporate feedback from users and stakeholders?
column6: Feedback is rarely collected or acted upon.
column7: Feedback is collected but slow to act upon.
column8: Feedback is regularly incorporated into development.
column9: Feedback loops are tight; user insights drive rapid iteration.

column4: How comprehensive is the team's automated testing coverage (unit, integration, E2E)?
column6: Minimal testing; automation is absent or broken.
column7: Some tests exist; coverage is incomplete.
column8: Good test coverage; automation is reliable.
column9: Comprehensive test coverage (unit, integration, E2E); automation runs fast and reliably.

column4: How effectively does the team address skill gaps in React, TypeScript, testing, and accessibility?
column6: Skill gaps are not identified or addressed.
column7: Skill gaps are known but improvements are slow.
column8: Skill gaps are being closed through training and mentorship.
column9: All critical skills are strong; team is proactive in closing emerging gaps.

column4: How well does the Frontend Head lead the team through technical changes (e.g., Vite, component library updates)?
column6: Changes are forced without explanation; team is resistant.
column7: Changes are introduced but adoption is slow and uneven.
column8: Changes are well-planned and adopted with minimal disruption.
column9: Changes are framed compellingly; team embraces evolution and leads improvements.

column4: Do team members have the autonomy to make decisions and take ownership of their work?
column6: Decisions are micromanaged; team has no autonomy.
column7: Team has limited autonomy; approval is often required.
column8: Team members are empowered to make most decisions.
column9: Team members are fully empowered; ownership is distributed and celebrated.

column3: Culture of Experimentation & Creative Problem Solving
column4: Does the team embrace experimentation and creative approaches to frontend challenges?
column6: Team follows existing patterns; no innovation or experimentation.
column7: Occasional experiments; risk-aversion limits creativity.
column8: Team regularly experiments and tries new approaches.
column9: Team proactively experiments; learning from failures is valued and celebrated.
